title: Number Systems & Base Conversions
content_markdown:
## Number Systems

Digital circuits process data in **binary (base-2)**. Other important bases:

| Base | Name | Digits |
|---|---|---|
| 2 | Binary | 0, 1 |
| 8 | Octal | 0–7 |
| 10 | Decimal | 0–9 |
| 16 | Hexadecimal | 0–9, A–F |

## Key Conversions

- **Decimal → Binary:** divide by 2, collect remainders bottom-up.
- **Binary → Hex:** group bits into nibbles of 4 from the right. e.g. `1101 0110` → `D6`.
- **Binary → Octal:** group into 3-bit sets. e.g. `110 101` → `65`.

## Signed Representations

- **Sign-magnitude:** MSB is sign bit; two representations of zero.
- **1's complement:** invert all bits; still two zeros.
- **2's complement:** invert all bits + 1; single zero, used in all modern hardware.

Range for n-bit 2's complement: −2^(n−1) to 2^(n−1)−1.

## Special Codes

- **Gray code:** only one bit changes between consecutive values → used in encoders and FIFO pointers for CDC safety.
- **BCD:** each decimal digit stored as 4 bits (0000–1001 valid).
- **Excess-3:** BCD + 3, self-complementing.

> Interview tip: Know how to convert quickly between binary, hex, and decimal, and explain why 2's complement is universally preferred.

title: Boolean Algebra, De Morgan & Logic Gates
content_markdown:
## Boolean Algebra Laws

| Law | OR form | AND form |
|---|---|---|
| Identity | A+0=A | A·1=A |
| Null | A+1=1 | A·0=0 |
| Idempotent | A+A=A | A·A=A |
| Complement | A+A'=1 | A·A'=0 |
| Absorption | A+(A·B)=A | A·(A+B)=A |

## De Morgan's Theorem

- **(A·B)' = A' + B'** — break the bar, change the operation.
- **(A+B)' = A'·B'**

Key use: convert NAND/NOR into equivalent AND-OR-NOT forms.

## Universal Gates

- **NAND and NOR** are universal — any Boolean function can be built using only one type.
- XOR: odd-parity detector; used in adders, Gray code converters.

## Canonical Forms

- **SOP (Sum of Products):** sum of minterms where output = 1.
- **POS (Product of Sums):** product of maxterms where output = 0.

Both are equivalent representations; SOP is easier to implement with NAND gates.

> De Morgan's is the most commonly tested Boolean identity in VLSI interviews — practice until instinctive.

title: K-Map Minimization & Quine-McCluskey
content_markdown:
## Karnaugh Map (K-Map)

A visual method to minimize Boolean functions by grouping adjacent 1-cells.

**Rules:**
1. Group 1s in powers of 2: 1, 2, 4, 8, 16.
2. Larger groups → fewer literals in the product term.
3. Groups can wrap around edges (toroidal adjacency).
4. Use **don't-cares (X)** to form larger groups.
5. Each essential prime implicant must appear in at least one group that covers an otherwise uncovered minterm.

## Example

F(A,B,C,D) with minterms {0,1,2,3,8,9,10,11}:
- Group {0,1,2,3} = A'B' → simplifies to just A'B'
- Group {0,2,8,10} = B'D' → covers four corners
- Result: F = A'B' + B'D'

## Don't-Cares

Invalid input combinations (e.g. BCD digits 10–15) can be treated as 1 or 0 to maximize group size.

## Quine-McCluskey (Tabular) Method

Systematic alternative to K-maps for more than 4 variables:
1. List minterms in binary, group by number of 1s.
2. Merge groups that differ by exactly one bit (mark the differing bit with –).
3. Repeat until no further merging possible.
4. Select a minimal cover of prime implicants.

> K-maps handle up to 5–6 variables practically; Quine-McCluskey is algorithm-friendly for CAD tools.

title: Adders, MUX, Encoder, Decoder & Comparator
content_markdown:
## Adders

| Type | Description |
|---|---|
| Half Adder | 1-bit: Sum = A⊕B, Carry = A·B |
| Full Adder | 1-bit + carry-in: Sum = A⊕B⊕Cin |
| Ripple Carry | Chain full adders; carry ripples O(n) |
| Carry Look-ahead | Computes carry in parallel using G (generate) and P (propagate) |

**BCD Adder:** adds 6 when sum > 9 to correct for decimal carry.

## Multiplexer (MUX)

A **2^n:1 MUX** selects one of 2^n inputs using n select lines. Any Boolean function of n variables can be implemented with a 2^(n−1):1 MUX (one variable used as data, rest as selects).

## Demultiplexer (DEMUX)

Routes one input to one of 2^n outputs — inverse of MUX.

## Encoder / Decoder

- **Encoder:** 2^n inputs → n-bit binary code. Priority encoder handles multiple simultaneous inputs.
- **Decoder:** n-bit input → 2^n outputs (only one active). Implements any minterm.

## Magnitude Comparator

Compares A and B: outputs A>B, A=B, A<B. Built from XOR gates (detect equality) and subtractor logic for inequality.

## Static Hazards

A **static-1 hazard** is a glitch to 0 on an output that should stay 1 during a transition. Eliminated by adding a **consensus term** to bridge the gap in the K-map.

> Know the full adder truth table and how to build a 4-bit ripple-carry adder from 4 full adders — classic breadboard and interview question.

title: Flip-Flops, Shift Registers & Counters
content_markdown:
## Flip-Flop Types

| FF | Characteristic |
|---|---|
| SR | S=1 sets, R=1 resets, S=R=1 is forbidden |
| D | Q follows D on clock edge; simplest, most used |
| JK | J=K=1 toggles; resolves SR forbidden state |
| T | Toggle on T=1; divide-by-2 frequency counter |

**Conversions:** any FF can be implemented from another by adding combinational logic between inputs.

## Latches vs Flip-Flops

- **Latch:** level-sensitive (transparent when enable=1).
- **Flip-flop:** edge-triggered (captures only at clock edge). RTL uses flops.

## Shift Registers

| Mode | In | Out |
|---|---|---|
| SISO | Serial | Serial |
| SIPO | Serial | Parallel |
| PISO | Parallel | Serial |
| PIPO | Parallel | Parallel |

A **Universal Shift Register** supports all four modes via a mode-select input.

## Counters

- **Ripple (async):** FF output clocks the next FF → MSB has cumulative delay.
- **Synchronous:** all FFs share the same clock → faster, no ripple delay.
- **Ring counter:** one '1' bit circulates through n FFs → n states.
- **Johnson counter:** inverted output fed back → 2n states.

Designing for a specific sequence: build state table → K-map excitation equations → implement.

> Synchronous counters are preferred in synthesis; ripple counters cause timing issues across clock domains.

title: Gate, Dataflow & Behavioral Modeling
content_markdown:
## Three Abstraction Levels

### 1. Gate-Level Modeling
Describes circuits using primitive logic gates directly.
```verilog
module half_adder(input a, b, output sum, carry);
  xor x1(sum, a, b);
  and a1(carry, a, b);
endmodule
```
Most detailed level; rarely used for large designs but output of synthesis.

### 2. Dataflow Modeling
Uses `assign` (continuous assignment) to describe logic as expressions.
```verilog
module half_adder(input a, b, output sum, carry);
  assign sum   = a ^ b;
  assign carry = a & b;
endmodule
```
Good for combinational logic; the synthesizer maps expressions to gates.

### 3. Behavioral Modeling
Describes *what* the circuit does, not *how*. Uses `always` and `initial` blocks.
```verilog
module half_adder(input a, b, output reg sum, carry);
  always @(*) begin
    sum   = a ^ b;
    carry = a & b;
  end
endmodule
```

## Choosing a Level

| Level | When to use |
|---|---|
| Gate | Examine synthesis output; drive strength analysis |
| Dataflow | Combinational assign logic |
| Behavioral | Sequential logic, FSMs, testbenches |

> All three levels can be mixed in the same design — use behavioral for RTL and let synthesis produce the gate netlist.

title: Verilog Data Types: wire, reg, Numbers & Operators
content_markdown:
## 4-State Values

| Value | Meaning |
|---|---|
| 0 | Logic zero / false |
| 1 | Logic one / true |
| X | Unknown (uninitialised or conflict) |
| Z | High-impedance (tristate / floating) |

## Nets vs Registers

- **wire (net):** continuously driven; used with `assign` or module ports. Default value: Z.
- **reg:** holds value between assignments; used inside `always`/`initial`. **Does NOT necessarily infer a physical register** — depends on context.

```verilog
wire a, b, y;
assign y = a & b;   // continuous

reg q;
always @(posedge clk) q <= d;  // infers a flop
```

## Number Literals

Format: `<size>'<base><value>`
- `4'b1010` — 4-bit binary = 10
- `8'hFF`  — 8-bit hex = 255
- `12'd100` — 12-bit decimal = 100
- Default size = 32 bits, default base = decimal.

## Operator Categories

- **Arithmetic:** + − * / %
- **Bitwise:** & | ^ ~ ^~ (XNOR)
- **Logical:** && || !
- **Shift:** << >> (logical), <<< >>> (arithmetic)
- **Concatenation:** `{a, b}` — combine bits
- **Replication:** `{4{1'b0}}` = `4'b0000`
- **Ternary:** `sel ? a : b`

> `reg` does NOT mean register — it's just a variable type in Verilog; context (combinational vs sequential `always`) determines what hardware is inferred.

title: always Block, Sensitivity List & Blocking vs Non-Blocking
content_markdown:
## The always Block

An `always` block executes repeatedly based on its sensitivity list. All `always` blocks run concurrently in simulation.

```verilog
// Combinational — sensitivity list is *
always @(*) begin
  y = a & b;   // blocking =
end

// Sequential — sensitive to clock edge
always @(posedge clk or negedge rst_n) begin
  if (!rst_n) q <= 1'b0;  // non-blocking <=
  else        q <= d;
end
```

## Blocking (=) vs Non-Blocking (<=)

| | Blocking `=` | Non-Blocking `<=` |
|---|---|---|
| Execution | Sequential (one after another) | Parallel (RHS evaluated first, LHS updated at end of timestep) |
| Use | Combinational logic | Sequential (clocked) logic |
| Race condition | Can cause races between flops | Prevents races — all flops update simultaneously |

## Golden Rules (Cummings)

1. Use `always @(*)` / blocking for combinational.
2. Use `always @(posedge clk)` / non-blocking for sequential.
3. **Never mix** blocking and non-blocking in the same always block.
4. Never make assignments to the same variable from two different always blocks.

## Inferred Latches

If a combinational `always` block does not assign an output on every possible path, the synthesizer infers a **latch** to retain the old value. Avoid with:
- Default assignments at the top of the block.
- Complete `if/else` and `case` with `default`.

> Inferred latches are a common synthesis bug — use `always_comb` in SystemVerilog to get a compile-time error instead.

title: Verilog Versions: 1995, 2001 & SystemVerilog
content_markdown:
## Evolution of Verilog

### Verilog-1995 (IEEE 1364-1995)
Original standard. Basic modules, wire/reg, always/initial blocks, gate primitives, and system tasks like `$display`.

### Verilog-2001 (IEEE 1364-2001)
Major improvements for readability and reuse:
- **ANSI-style port declarations** — declare type and direction in one line.
- **Automatic sensitivity list** `@(*)` — no manual listing.
- **Generate blocks** — parameterized structural replication.
- **Signed arithmetic** — `signed` keyword on reg/wire.
- **Multi-dimensional arrays.**

```verilog
// Verilog-2001 ANSI port style
module adder #(parameter W=8)(
  input  [W-1:0] a, b,
  output [W:0]   sum);
  assign sum = a + b;
endmodule
```

### SystemVerilog (IEEE 1800)
Extension of Verilog with:
- `logic` type (replaces wire/reg for single-driver nets)
- `always_comb`, `always_ff`, `always_latch` for intent
- OOP: classes, inheritance, polymorphism
- Constraints, coverage, assertions (verification features)
- Interfaces and clocking blocks

## Verilog vs VHDL

| | Verilog | VHDL |
|---|---|---|
| Syntax | C-like, concise | Ada-like, verbose |
| Industry use | ASIC/FPGA design | Defense/aerospace |
| Learning curve | Easier for beginners | Steeper |

> All modern RTL is written in SystemVerilog; Verilog-2001 style is still widely supported in older flows.

title: What is STA & How It Works
content_markdown:
## What is Static Timing Analysis?

STA validates timing performance by checking **all possible paths** for violations — without simulation vectors. It is:
- **Faster** than dynamic simulation (no need to run logic).
- **More thorough** (checks every path, not just exercised ones).
- **Limited to timing** — it cannot verify functionality.

## The Timing Path

Every path has:
- **Startpoint:** input port or a register clock pin (where data is launched).
- **Combinational logic network:** AND/OR/XOR/inverter elements with no memory.
- **Endpoint:** register data input pin or output port (where data is captured).

## Path Types

| Path Type | Description |
|---|---|
| Data path | Launch FF → capture FF through logic |
| Clock path | Clock source → clock pin of sequential element |
| Clock-gating path | Input → clock-gating enable |
| Asynchronous path | Input → async set/clear pin (recovery/removal check) |

## Delay Calculation

- **Cell delay:** input transition time + output load capacitance → looked up from a **delay table** in the cell library.
- **Net delay:** caused by parasitic RC of the interconnection wire.
- Total path delay = sum of all cell and net delays.

## STA vs Dynamic Simulation

| | STA | Dynamic Simulation |
|---|---|---|
| Speed | Very fast | Slow |
| Coverage | All paths | Only exercised paths |
| Can check functionality? | No | Yes |

> STA is the industry-standard timing sign-off method; dynamic simulation is used for functional verification.

title: Setup, Hold, Slack & Timing Violations
content_markdown:
## Setup Check (Max-Delay)

Data must arrive at the capture FF input **before** the clock edge by at least Tsetup.

```
Required: Tclk + Tskew
Arrival:  Tcq_launch + Tlogic + Tsetup
Setup slack = Required − Arrival
```

**Fixing setup violations:**
- Reduce logic depth (restructure or pipeline).
- Upsize cells or use faster library.
- Reduce clock uncertainty / improve CTS.
- Relax clock (lower frequency).

## Hold Check (Min-Delay)

Data must remain stable **after** the capture clock edge for at least Thold.

```
Required (min): Thold + Tskew
Arrival (min):  Tcq_min + Tlogic_min
Hold slack = Arrival_min − Required_min
```

**Fixing hold violations:**
- Add delay buffers on the short path.
- **Slowing the clock does NOT help hold** — the check is independent of period.

## Slack

- **Positive slack:** timing met.
- **Negative slack:** timing violation.
- **WNS (Worst Negative Slack):** most critical single endpoint.
- **TNS (Total Negative Slack):** sum of all negative slacks — reflects breadth of violations.

## Corner Analysis

STA is run at **process-voltage-temperature (PVT) corners**:
- **Slow corner (SS, low V, high T):** worst for setup.
- **Fast corner (FF, high V, low T):** worst for hold.

> One-liner: *Setup is a clock-period problem (slow clock helps). Hold is a data-race problem (adding delay helps).*

title: Timing Exceptions: False Path, Multicycle Path & OCV
content_markdown:
## Timing Exceptions (SDC)

Paths that should NOT be analyzed with the default single-cycle setup/hold assumption need explicit exceptions — otherwise the tool reports false violations.

### False Path (`set_false_path`)
A path that is **never functionally active** at the same time:
- Asynchronous clock domain crossings (properly synchronized).
- Static configuration paths.
- Opposite-mode paths (scan vs functional).

```tcl
set_false_path -from [get_clocks clk_a] -to [get_clocks clk_b]
```

### Multicycle Path (`set_multicycle_path`)
Data that legitimately takes more than one cycle to propagate:
```tcl
# Setup: data has 2 cycles (capture on 2nd edge)
set_multicycle_path 2 -setup -from [get_pins u_slow/q]
# Also loosen hold back by 1 cycle
set_multicycle_path 1 -hold  -from [get_pins u_slow/q]
```

### On-Chip Variation (OCV / AOCV / POCV)
Process variation means launch and capture paths see different delays even from the same cell. OCV applies a **derating factor** (or statistical distribution in POCV) to capture this. Makes setup harder and hold easier.

## Common SDC Commands

```tcl
create_clock -name clk -period 2.0 [get_ports clk]
set_input_delay  0.3 -clock clk [get_ports din]
set_output_delay 0.2 -clock clk [get_ports dout]
set_clock_uncertainty 0.1 [get_clocks clk]
```

> Misapplied false paths and MCPs are a leading cause of silicon timing bugs — apply them only when the logic intent truly justifies them.

title: What is SystemVerilog & Why It Replaced Verilog
content_markdown:
## SystemVerilog (IEEE 1800)

SystemVerilog is an **extension of Verilog** with features for both design (RTL) and verification. It became the dominant language because:

- Verilog alone was insufficient for verifying complex designs.
- Adds **OOP** (classes, inheritance, polymorphism) for testbench construction.
- Adds **constrained random stimulus** and **functional coverage**.
- Adds **assertions (SVA)** for property checking.

## RTL Improvements Over Verilog

### `logic` type
Replaces both `wire` and `reg` for single-driver nets — avoids confusion:
```systemverilog
logic [7:0] data;  // can be driven by assign or always
```

### Procedural blocks with intent
```systemverilog
always_comb  // combinational — compiler checks no latches
always_ff @(posedge clk or negedge rst_n)  // sequential
always_latch // explicit latch intent
```

### Rich type system
- `bit` — 2-state (faster simulation, no X/Z)
- `byte`, `shortint`, `int`, `longint` — C-compatible integer types
- `enum` — named states for FSMs
- `struct` — group related signals
- `typedef` — reusable type aliases

## `unique` and `priority` case

```systemverilog
unique case (sel)  // all values covered, no overlap → tool warns on gaps
  2'b00: y = a;
  2'b01: y = b;
  2'b10: y = c;
  2'b11: y = d;
endcase
```

> Use `always_comb` and `always_ff` in all new RTL — they give earlier error detection and clearer synthesis intent.

title: Interfaces, Modports & Clocking Blocks
Classes, Inheritance & Randomization in SystemVerilog
content_markdown:
## Classes

SystemVerilog OOP enables reusable, extensible testbench components.

```systemverilog
class Packet;
  rand bit [7:0] addr;
  rand bit [7:0] data;
  rand bit       valid;

  constraint c_addr { addr inside {[8'h00:8'hFF]}; }
  constraint c_data { valid -> data != 0; }  // implication
endclass
```

## Randomization

- `rand` — randomly assigned each time `randomize()` is called.
- `randc` — cycles through all values before repeating.
- `randomize()` returns 1 on success, 0 on failure (constraint conflict).

## Inheritance

```systemverilog
class LargePacket extends Packet;
  constraint c_big { data > 8'h80; }  // adds extra constraint
endclass
```

- `virtual` methods enable **polymorphism** — the right derived method is called at runtime.
- `super.method()` calls the parent class method.

## Key OOP Concepts for UVM

| Concept | SystemVerilog | UVM use |
|---|---|---|
| Class | `class Foo;` | All UVM components |
| Inheritance | `class Bar extends Foo` | Customize base classes |
| Virtual methods | `virtual task run` | Polymorphic phases |
| Factory | `type_id::create()` | Swap components at runtime |

> Solid OOP in SystemVerilog is a prerequisite for UVM — master classes, inheritance, and virtual methods first.

title: What is UVM & Why It Became the Standard
content_markdown:
## UVM — Universal Verification Methodology

UVM (IEEE 1800.2) is the industry-standard framework for verifying digital designs and SoCs. It provides a **class library and methodology** for building modular, reusable, scalable verification environments in SystemVerilog.

## Key Characteristics

- **Standardised:** IEEE 1800.2 ensures interoperability across all major EDA tools.
- **Modular:** pre-built base classes (driver, monitor, scoreboard) accelerate development.
- **Reusable:** verification IP (VIP) can be shared across projects and organisations.
- **Configurable:** factory pattern and `config_db` allow flexible testbench adaptation.

## Evolution: OVM → UVM

| | OVM (2008) | UVM (2011) |
|---|---|---|
| Standard | No formal standard | IEEE 1800.2 |
| Flexibility | TLM-only focus | Supports TLM + RTL + mixed-signal |
| Reusability | Tight TB–DUT coupling | Self-contained agents |
| Maintainability | Breaking changes common | Deprecation warnings; backward compatible |

OVM is now obsolete — all modern projects use UVM.

## Why It Matters

A modern SoC verification environment can have 200,000+ lines of UVM code, with 50+ reusable agents for protocols like PCIe, AXI, DDR. Without standardisation, teams would rebuild equivalent infrastructure for every project.

## UVM Versions

- UVM 1.0 (2011) — initial release
- UVM 1.1 (2013) — register sequences, messaging
- UVM 1.2 (2014) — phasing enhancements
- UVM 2020 (IEEE 1800.2-2020) — current standard

> UVM = infrastructure, not magic. It solves the problem of rebuilding the same testbench skeleton for every project.

title: UVM Testbench Components: Driver, Monitor, Scoreboard & Agent
content_markdown:
## Core Component Hierarchy

```
uvm_test
  └─ uvm_env
       └─ uvm_agent  (one per protocol interface)
            ├─ uvm_driver      ← drives DUT inputs
            ├─ uvm_monitor     ← observes DUT outputs
            └─ uvm_sequencer   ← arbitrates transactions
       └─ uvm_scoreboard   ← checks correctness
```

## Each Component's Role

- **`uvm_driver`:** converts transaction objects into pin-level DUT stimulus.
- **`uvm_monitor`:** observes DUT signals passively and reconstructs transactions.
- **`uvm_scoreboard`:** compares actual DUT behavior against expected (reference model).
- **`uvm_agent`:** groups driver + monitor + sequencer for one protocol interface; can be **active** (drives) or **passive** (observes only).
- **`uvm_env`:** top-level container for all agents and scoreboards.
- **`uvm_test`:** defines the test scenario; overrides factory objects; controls simulation flow.

## Transactions (`uvm_sequence_item`)

```systemverilog
class my_txn extends uvm_sequence_item;
  rand bit [31:0] addr;
  rand bit [31:0] data;
  rand bit        write;
  `uvm_object_utils_begin(my_txn)
    `uvm_field_int(addr, UVM_ALL_ON)
    `uvm_field_int(data, UVM_ALL_ON)
  `uvm_object_utils_end
endclass
```

Transactions abstract protocol detail — your scoreboard works at transaction level, not pin level.

> Always write agents as self-contained, reusable VIP. A good agent can be dropped into any project that uses the same protocol.

title: UVM Phases, Objections & config_db
content_markdown:
## UVM Phases

Phases enforce deterministic testbench construction and synchronization:

| Phase | Type | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `build_phase` | Function | Construct components top-down |
| `connect_phase` | Function | Wire TLM ports between components |
| `end_of_elaboration_phase` | Function | Final checks before simulation |
| `start_of_simulation_phase` | Function | Print topology, initial config |
| `run_phase` | Task (time-consuming) | Drive stimulus, monitor outputs |
| `extract_phase` | Function | Collect coverage |
| `check_phase` | Function | Verify pass/fail |
| `report_phase` | Function | Print summary |

## Objections

The `run_phase` ends when **all objections are dropped**. A component raises an objection to keep simulation running, then drops it when done:
```systemverilog
task run_phase(uvm_phase phase);
  phase.raise_objection(this);
  // ... run stimulus ...
  phase.drop_objection(this);
endtask
```

## `uvm_config_db`

Passes virtual interfaces and configuration objects down the component hierarchy without tight coupling:
```systemverilog
// In top-level testbench
uvm_config_db #(virtual apb_if)::set(null, "uvm_test_top.*", "vif", apb_vif);

// In the driver/agent
uvm_config_db #(virtual apb_if)::get(this, "", "vif", m_vif);
```

> Always call `super.build_phase(phase)` — forgetting it is one of the top causes of mysterious UVM crashes in beginners.

title: UVM Factory, Sequences & Register Abstraction Layer
What is Physical Design & The Full Chip Design Journey
content_markdown:
## Physical Design Definition

Physical design transforms a **logic design** (gate-level netlist) into an actual **layout** that can be manufactured on a silicon wafer.

## The Full Design Journey

| Step | Description |
|---|---|
| Architectural Design | High-level architecture, microarchitecture decisions |
| Logic Design | RTL coding → synthesis → gate-level netlist |
| **Physical Design** | Place and route the netlist on silicon |
| Analysis & Verification | PPA checks, DRC, LVS, STA signoff |
| Tape Out & Fabrication | Send GDSII to foundry |

## What Physical Design Produces

The goal is to produce a **GDSII file** — the layout database — that represents:
- All standard cell and macro placements.
- All metal wire routes on multiple layers.
- Power and ground distribution network.
- Clock tree topology.

This layout must satisfy:
- **Timing:** all setup/hold checks must pass at signoff corners.
- **Power:** supply voltage and current must be adequate (IR drop and EM checks).
- **Manufacturability:** foundry design rules (DRC) must not be violated.
- **Correct-by-construction:** layout must match the netlist (LVS).

## Key Challenge

For a modern SoC with billions of gates, it is computationally infeasible to do PD manually. EDA tools automate placement, routing, CTS and optimization while respecting designer-specified constraints (timing, congestion, area).

> A good floorplan is the single most important factor in whether the rest of the PD flow converges on time and within PPA targets.

title: Floorplanning, Placement, CTS, Routing & Power Planning
content_markdown:
## Step-by-Step PD Flow

### 1. Floorplanning
Defines the die/core area and places large blocks (memory macros, IPs, IO pads). Goals: minimize wire length for critical paths, allow clean power delivery, avoid congestion.

### 2. Power Planning
Builds the **power distribution network (PDN):** rings around the core + straps across the die. Ensures every cell's VDD/VSS pins are within acceptable IR drop of the supply pads.

### 3. Placement
Places all standard cells within the core area to minimise wire length and meet timing. Modern tools use **timing-driven placement** — cells on critical paths get better positions.

### 4. Clock Tree Synthesis (CTS)
Builds a balanced **clock tree** — a network of buffers/inverters that distributes the clock to every sequential element with minimised **skew** and controlled **latency**. CTS is done after placement so the physical wire capacitances are known.

### 5. Routing
Connects all nets with metal wires on multiple routing layers:
- **Global routing:** assigns routes to channels.
- **Detailed routing:** exact wire shapes, respecting min-width/spacing rules.

## Key Design Concerns

| Issue | Cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| Congestion | Too many wires in one area | Spread cells, reserve channels |
| IR drop | Resistance in power grid | Add straps, widen power rails |
| EM (Electromigration) | High current in thin metal | Upsize wires, add vias |
| Crosstalk / SI | Capacitive coupling | Increase spacing, add shielding |

> CTS happens after placement and before routing — never route signal nets before the clock tree is inserted.

title: Physical Verification: DRC, LVS, STA & IR Signoff
content_markdown:
## Physical Design Signoff Checklist

Before tape-out, every check below must pass at the required PVT corners.

### DRC — Design Rule Check
Verifies the layout obeys the foundry's **geometric manufacturing rules**:
- Minimum metal width and spacing.
- Via enclosure rules.
- Cell density limits.
- Antenna rules (gate oxide damage from metal etching).

DRC violations → layout cannot be fabricated or will yield poorly.

### LVS — Layout Versus Schematic
Verifies that the physical connections in the layout **match the gate-level netlist** exactly. Catches:
- Open nets (missing connection).
- Short circuits (two nets connected).
- Extra or missing components.

### STA Signoff
**Static Timing Analysis** at slow (setup) and fast (hold) corners across all paths:
- All setup slacks ≥ 0 at worst PVT corner.
- All hold slacks ≥ 0 at best PVT corner.
- IR-drop-aware STA accounts for supply sag reducing cell speed.

### IR Drop & EM
- **IR drop:** voltage loss across power grid → cells near supply starve → timing failure. Must be < 5–10% of Vdd typically.
- **Electromigration:** metal atoms drift under high current → eventual wire failure. Check current density against EM limits.

## ECO (Engineering Change Order)
A late change to fix a bug or meet a timing target without re-running the full flow — small incremental changes to placement, routing, or cell sizing.

> DRC + LVS must be clean, STA must be met, and IR/EM must pass before tapeout — no exceptions.

title: AMBA Protocol Family: APB, AHB & AXI Compared
content_markdown:
## AMBA — The On-Chip Bus Standard

The Advanced Microcontroller Bus Architecture (AMBA) family forms the backbone of most SoC on-chip interconnects.

## The Three Main Protocols

| Feature | APB | AHB | AXI |
|---|---|---|---|
| Max Throughput | Low | Medium | Very High |
| Pipeline Support | No | Yes | Advanced |
| Burst Length | N/A | Up to 16 | Up to 256 |
| Outstanding Transactions | 1 | 1 | Multiple |
| Power Consumption | Lowest | Medium | Higher |
| Design Complexity | Simple | Medium | Complex |
| Area Overhead | Minimal | Medium | Significant |

## When to Choose Which

**Choose APB when:**
- Interfacing simple peripherals (GPIO, timers, UARTs, I2C)
- Power is critical
- Bandwidth < ~100 MHz

**Choose AHB when:**
- Moderate performance; memory controllers, DMA
- Need burst transfers
- Legacy IP compatibility required

**Choose AXI when:**
- High-performance applications (CPUs, GPUs, memory controllers)
- Need multiple outstanding transactions
- Out-of-order completion needed
- Modern ARM-based designs

## Bridge Pattern

In a typical SoC: CPU ↔ AXI interconnect ↔ AXI-to-AHB bridge ↔ AHB ↔ AHB-to-APB bridge ↔ APB peripherals.

> Use the lightest protocol that meets your bandwidth requirement — mixing heavier protocols where not needed wastes area and power.

title: APB & AHB: Signals, Transactions & Timing
content_markdown:
## APB — Advanced Peripheral Bus

**Two-phase protocol:** SETUP phase → ACCESS phase.

Key signals:
- `PCLK`, `PRESETn` — clock and reset
- `PADDR` — address
- `PWDATA` / `PRDATA` — write/read data
- `PSEL` — select (asserted in SETUP)
- `PENABLE` — asserted in ACCESS phase
- `PWRITE` — direction
- `PREADY` — slave inserts wait states by de-asserting
- `PSLVERR` — slave error response

**Transaction sequence:** assert PSEL (SETUP cycle) → assert PENABLE (ACCESS cycle) → transfer completes when PREADY=1.

## AHB — Advanced High-performance Bus

**Pipelined:** address phase of one transfer overlaps with data phase of the previous.

Key signals:
- `HCLK`, `HRESETn`
- `HADDR` — address (registered)
- `HWDATA` / `HRDATA` — write/read data
- `HTRANS` — IDLE / BUSY / NONSEQ / SEQ
- `HBURST` — SINGLE / INCR / WRAP4 / INCR4 etc.
- `HREADY` — transfer complete (slave inserts wait states)
- `HRESP` — OKAY / ERROR

**Burst types:** INCR (incrementing), WRAP (wrapping address), FIXED. Burst count encodes in HBURST.

## AHB vs APB at a Glance

```
APB: IDLE → SETUP → ACCESS → IDLE
AHB: ADDR phase | DATA phase (pipelined)
```

> APB is non-pipelined by design (simplicity goal). AHB pipelining means the master presents the next address while the current data phase is still completing.

title: AXI4: Five Channels, Handshake, Bursts & Rules
content_markdown:
## AXI4 — Five Independent Channels

| Channel | Direction | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| AW (Write Address) | Master → Slave | Write address + burst info |
| W (Write Data) | Master → Slave | Write data + strobes |
| B (Write Response) | Slave → Master | Completion status |
| AR (Read Address) | Master → Slave | Read address + burst info |
| R (Read Data) | Slave → Master | Read data + status |

Each channel uses a **VALID/READY handshake** — transfer occurs only when both are high simultaneously.

## Key AXI Rules

1. **VALID must NOT wait for READY** — a master cannot withhold VALID until it sees READY (prevents deadlock).
2. **READY may wait for VALID** — a slave can choose to accept only after seeing VALID.
3. A burst must not cross a **4 KB address boundary**.
4. `AWLEN` = number of beats − 1 (so 0 = single transfer, 255 = 256-beat burst).

## Burst Types

| AWBURST/ARBURST | Name | Address pattern |
|---|---|---|
| 2'b00 | FIXED | Same address each beat (FIFOs) |
| 2'b01 | INCR | Incrementing (normal memory) |
| 2'b10 | WRAP | Incrementing with wrap (caches) |

## Outstanding Transactions

AXI allows multiple transactions in-flight simultaneously using **IDs** (`AWID`, `ARID`). Transactions with different IDs may complete **out of order**; same-ID transactions must complete in order.

## AXI Variants

- **AXI4:** full features (used for memory-mapped IP)
- **AXI4-Lite:** simplified (fixed 1-beat, no burst — for registers)
- **AXI4-Stream:** data-only, no address (streaming data paths)
- **AXI-ACE:** adds cache coherency for multi-core

> VALID must not depend on READY — this rule is the most commonly violated in AXI implementations and leads to deadlock.
